title: 第 30 節
en: Rv. I. 164,4-6. ‘Who has seen the First-born, when He who had no bones, i.e. no form, bore him that had bones. The life, the blood, and the soul of the earth— where are they? Who went to ask it to one who knew it? Simple-minded, not comprehending it in my mind, I ask for the hidden places of the gods. ... Ignorant I ask the knowing sages, that I, the not-knowing, may know, what is the One in the form of the Unborn which has settled these six spaces.’
Still stronger is this confession as repeated again and again in the Upanishads.
For instance, Svet. Up. 1V.19. ‘No one has grasped Him above, or across, or in the middle. There is no likeness of Him whose name is Great Glory.’
Or, Mund. Up. III. 1,8. ‘He is not apprehended by the eye, nor by speech, nor by the other senses, not by penance or good works.’

Ken. Up. I. 8. ‘Thy eye does not go thither, nor speech, nor mind. We do not know, we do not understand, how any one can teach it. It is different from the known, it is also above the unknown, thus we have heard from those of old who taught us this.’
Khand. Up. IV. 3, 6. ‘Mortals see Him not, though He dwells in many places.’

In the Taitt. Up. 11. 4, it is said that words turn back from it with the mind, without having reached it—and in another place, Kath. Up. III. 15, it is distinctly called nameless, intangible, formless, imperishable. And again, Mund. Up. I, 1, 6, invisible, and not to be grasped.

These very doubts and perplexities are most touch- TRUE IMMORTALITY. 239 ing. I doubt whether we find anything like them anywhere else. On one point only these ancient searchers after God seem to have no doubt whatever, namely, that this Being is one and without a second. We saw it when the poet said, ‘That which is one the poets eall it in many ways, and in the Upanishads, this One without a second becomes a constant name of the Supreme Being. Thus the Kath. Up. V. 12, says: ‘There is one ruler, the soul within all things, who makes the one form manifold. And the SvetasvataraUp. VI. 11, adds: ‘He is the one God, hidden in all things, all-pervading, the soul within all beings, watching over all works, dwelling in all, the witness, the perceiver, the only one, free from all qualities, He is the one ruler of many who (seem to act, but really) do not act.’

The hand. Up. VI. 2, 1, says: ‘In the beginning there was that only which is, one only, without a second ;’ and the Brzh. Ar. Up. IV. 3, 32, adds: ‘That one seer (subject) is an ocean, and without any duality.’

Mund. Up. II. 2,5. ‘In Him the heaven, the earth, and the sky are woven, the mind also with all the senses. Know Him alone as the Self, and leave off other names. He is the bridge of the Immortal, i.e. the bridge by which we reach our own immortality.’
These are mere gropings, gropings in the dark, no doubt; but even thus, where do we see such gropings after God except in India ?

The human mind, however, cannot long go on without names, and some of the names given to the One Unknowable and Unnameable Being, which we shall now have to examine, have caused and are still causing great difficulty.
Name for the highest Godhead, Brahman.

One of the best-known names is Brahman, originally a neuter, but used often promiscuously as a masculine also. It would be an immense help if we were certain of the etymology of Brahman. We should then know, what is always most important, its first conception, for it is clear, and philosophers ought by this time to have learnt it, that every word must have meant at first that which it means etymologically. Many attempts have been made to discover the etymology of Brahman, but neither that nor the successive growth of its meanings can be ascertained with perfect certainty. It has been supposed? that certain passages in the KathaUpanishad (11. 13; VI. 17) were meant to imply a derivation of brahman from the root barh or brih, to tear off, as if brahman meant at first what was torn off or separated, absolutum ; but there is no other evidence for the existence of this line of thought in India. Others have derived brahman from the root barh or brzh, in the sense of swelling or growing. Thus Dr. Haug, in his paper on Brahman und die Brahmanen, published in 1871, supposed that brahman must have meant originally what grows, and he saw a proof of this in the corresponding Zend word Baresman (Barsom), a bundle of twigs (virgae) used by the priests, particularly at the Izeshan sacrifices. He then assigns to brahman the more abstract meaning of growth and welfare, and what causes growth and welfare, namely, sacred songs. In this way he holds that brahman came to mean the Veda, the holy word. Lastly, he assigns to brahman the meaning of 1 Deussen, Vedanta, p. 128.

TRUE IMMORTALITY. 241 force as manifested in nature, and that of universal force, or the Supreme Being, that which, according to Sankara, ‘is eternal, pure, intelligent, free, omniscient and omnipotent.’

When by a well-known grammatical process this neuter bréhman (nom. brahma) is changed into the masculine brahmén (nom. brahma), it comes to mean a@ man conversant with Brahman, a member of the priestly caste; secondly, a priest charged with the special duty of superintending the sacrifice, but likewise the personal creator, the universal force conceived as a personal god, the same as Pragapati, and in later times one of the Trimirti, Brahman, Vishnu, and Siva. So far Dr. Haug.

Dr. Muir, in his Sanskrit Texts, i. p. 240, starts from bréhman in the sense of prayer, hymn, while he takes the derivative masculine brahman as meaning one who prays, a poet or sage, then a priest in general, and lastly a priest charged with special duties.

Professor Roth also takes the original sense of Brahman to have been prayer, not, however, praise or thanksgiving, but that kind of invocation which, with the force of will directed to the god, desires to draw him to the worshipper, and to obtain satisfaction from him.

I must confess that the hymns of the Veda, as we now read them, are hardly so full of fervent devotion that they could well be called outbursts. And there always remains the question why the creative force of the universe should have been called by the same name. It seems to me that the idea of creative force or propelling power might well have been expressed by (4) R 242 LECTURE VIII.

Brahman, as derived from a root barh!, to break forth. or to drive forth; but the other brahman, before it came to mean hymn or prayer, seems to have had the more general meaning of speech or word. There are indeed a few indications left to show that the root barh had the meaning of uttering or speaking. Brihas-pati, who is also called Brahmamnas-pati, is often explained as Vasas-pati, the lord of speech, so that brzh seems to have been a synonym of vak. But what is still more important is that the Latin verbum, as I pointed out many years ago, can be traced back letter by letter to the same root. Nay, if we accept vridh as a parallel form of vrih, the English word also can claim the same origin. It would seem therefore that bréhman meant originally utterance, word, and then only hymn, and the sacred word, the Veda, while when it is used in the sense of creative force, it would have been conceived originally as that which utters or throws forth or manifests. Tempting as it is, we can hardly suppose that the ancient framers of the Sanskrit language had any suspicion of the identity of the Logos prophorikés and endidthetos of the Stoies, or of the world as word or thought, the Logos of the Creator. But that they had some recollection of brahman having originally meant word, can be proved by several passages from the Veda. I do not attach any importance to such passages as Brvh. Arve 129, vag vai Brahma, speech is Brahma, for Brahman is here in the same way identified with prava, breath,manas mind, aditya, sun, and many other things. But when we ' Brahma is sometimes combined with brihat, growing or great, see Svet. Up. III. 7.

TRUE IMMORTALITY. 243 read, Rv. 1. 164, 35, Brahma ayém vakih paramdim vyéma, what can be the meaning of Brahma mase. being called here the highest heaven, or, it may be, the highest woof, of speech, if there had not been some connection between bréhman and val:? There is another important passage in a hymn addressed to Brihaspati, the lord of speech, where we read, X. 71,1: “Ὁ Brihaspati (lord of brih or speech), when men, giving names, sent forth the first beginning of speech, then whatever was best and faultless in them, hidden within them, became manifested through desire. I believe therefore that the word brahman had a double history, one beginning with brahman, as neuter, τὸ ὄντως ὄν, the propelling force of the universe, and leading on to Brahman, mase., as the creator of the world, who causes all things to burst forth, later one of the Hindu Triad or Trim{rti, eonsisting of Brahman, Siva, and Vishnu; the other beginning with bréh-man, word or utterance, and gradually restricted to brahman, hymn of praise, accompanied by sacrificial offerings, and then, with change of gender and accent, brahman, he who utters, prays, and sacrifices, a member of the priestly caste.

Brahman, even when used as a neuter, is often followed by masculiné forms. And there are many passages where it must remain doubtful whether Brahman was conceived as an impersonal force, or as a personal being, nay, as both at the same time. Thus we read, Taitt. Up. III. 1,1: ‘ That from whence these beings are born, that by which when born they live, that into which they enter at their death, try to know that, that is Brahman.’
In the Atharva-veda X. 2, 25, we read: ‘By whom R2 244. LECTURE VIII.
was this earth ordered, by whom was the upper sky created? By whom was this uplifted?’ &e.
The answer is : ‘ By Brahma was this earth ordered,’ &e.
Sometimes Brahman is identified with Prana, breath, as in Brih. Ar. Up. III. (s), 9,9: ‘He asked, who is the one God? Yag/avalkya replied: Breath or spirit, and he is Brahman.’

Sometimes again it is said that Prana, spirit, arose from Brahman, as when we read, Mund. Up. IL. 1, 8: ‘Brahman swells by means of heat; hence is produced food (or matter), from food breath (prana), mind, &e.

However, this Brahman is only one out of many names, each representing an attempt to arrive at the concept of a Supreme Being, free, as much as possible, from all mythological elements, free from purely human qualities, free also from sex or gender.
Purusha.

Another of these names is Purusha, which means originally man or person. Thus we read, Mund. Up. II. 1, 1-38: ‘ As from a blazing fire sparks, being like fire, fly forth a thousandfold, thus are various beings brought forth from the Imperishable, and return thither also, That heavenly Person (Purusha) is without body, he is both within and without, not produced, without breath and without mind, higher than the high, imperishable. From him is born breath, (spirit), mind, and all organs of sense, ether, air, light, water, and the earth, the support of all.’
zh: 梨俱吠陀第一卷第164頌第4-6節。「誰曾見過長子，當那無骨者，即無形者，生出有骨者時。大地的生命、血液和靈魂——它們在何處？誰前去向知道的人請教？我心思簡樸，未能領悟，我請問諸神的隱秘之處。……我雖無知卻向智慧的聖賢請教，願我這無知者能得知那以非生者之形出現、安置此六重空間的唯一者。」
這種自白在《奧義書》中一次又一次地反覆出現，其力度更為強烈。
例如，《白色奧義書》（Svet. Up.）四.19：「沒有人能在上方、橫向或中間把握住祂。沒有人能比得上名叫『偉大榮耀』的祂。」
或見《蒙德卡奧義書》（Mund. Up.）III, 1,8：「他不被眼睛所領悟，亦不被言語所領悟，不被其他感官所領悟，不被苦行或善業所領悟。」
《肯那奧義書》(Ken. Up.) I. 8：「汝之眼不及彼處，言語亦不及，心識更不及。我等不知，不解如何有人能教示之。其異於所知之物，亦超乎未知之物，如是我等從昔日教示我等者聞之。」
《歌者奧義書》IV‧3‧6：「凡人不見祂，雖祂住於眾多之處。」

在《泰帝利耶奧義書》（Taitt. Up.）第二卷第四節中，有言詞與心智因未能臻至於它而退縮其後；在另一處，《卡塔奧義書》（Kath. Up.）第三卷第十五節則明確稱之為無名（nameless）、無形可觸（intangible）、無相（formless）、不壞（imperishable）。再者，《蒙德卡奧義書》（Mund. Up.）第一卷第一章第六節謂其為無可見（invisible）且不可把握（not to be grasped）。

這些疑惑和困頓本身極其動人。我懷疑在他處是否能找到類似的東西。在一點上，這些古代尋求上帝的人似乎毫無疑慮，那就是這個存在體是唯一的，沒有第二個。我們在詩人所言的「那唯一之物，詩人以多種方式稱呼它」中見過了，而在《奧義書》中，這個無第二者之唯一成為最高存在的恆定名稱。因此《卡塔奧義書》V. 12 云：「有一位統治者，存在於萬物之中的靈魂，他使唯一的形式成為多樣。」而《白雪奧義書》VI. 11 補充道：「他是唯一的神，隱藏於萬物之中，遍在者，存在於一切生物之中的靈魂，監看萬般作為，住於一切之中，見證者，知覺者，唯一者，超越一切性質，他是眾多者的唯一統治者，（似乎行動，但實則）不行動。」
《手奧義書》（Up. VI. 2, 1）云：「太初唯有此在者，一者而已，無有其二；」《白駬林奧義書》（Brzh. Ar. Up. IV. 3, 32）復云：「此一見者（主體）如海洋，無有任何二元性。」
《蒙德卡奧義書》II. 2,5：「在祂之中，天、地、空都織成一體，心識及一切感官亦然。當認識祂為自我（Ātman），而放棄其他名稱。祂是不死者之橋，即是我們達致自身不朽之橋。」
這些不過是摸索，無疑是在黑暗中的摸索；但即使如此，除了在印度，我們在何處見到對上帝如此執著的摸索呢？
然而，人心終究無法長期不賴名字而行，而我們現在必須檢視的那些給予獨一無二的不可知、不可名言之存有的名字中，有些名字已然導致並仍在導致巨大的困難。
至高神靈的名稱，梵天（Brahman）。

其中最為人所知的名字之一是梵天（Brahman），原本為中性詞，但也經常被混用為陽性詞。若我們能確定梵天的詞源，將會大有裨益。我們將能知道——這始終是最重要的——它最初的觀念，因為很明顯，哲學家到此時應當已經領悟，每個詞語起初必然具有其詞源上所具有的意義。許多學者曾試圖發現梵天的詞源，但無論是詞源本身或其義義的連續演變，都無法獲得完全確定的把握。有人推測《迦喇奧義書》（Katha-Upanishad）中的某些段落（II. 13；VI. 17）意圖暗示梵天源自詞根 barh 或 brih，即「撕離」之義，彷彿梵天最初意指被撕離或分離之物，即絕對者（absolutum）；然而，印度並無其他證據支持這一思想脈絡的存在。其他學者則將梵天源於詞根 barh 或 brzh，具有「膨脹」或「生長」之意。因此，豪格博士（Dr. Haug）在其 1871 年發表的《梵天與婆羅門》一文中推測，梵天最初必然意指「生長之物」，且他在相應的祆教語詞 Baresman（Barsom）中見到了此說的證明——此詞指的是一束柴枝（virgae），為祭司所使用，尤其在伊舍山祭祀（Izeshan sacrifices）時使用。他隨後將梵天賦予更為抽象的「生長與幸福」之義，以及「導致生長與幸福之物」，即聖歌。如此他認為梵天遂來表示吠陀，即聖言。最後，他賦予梵天以
真實的不朽性。241 自然中所顯現的力量，以及普遍的力量，或至高的存在——根據商羯羅（Sankara）的說法，那就是「永恆的、純淨的、具有智慧的、自由的、無所不知和無所不能的」。

當這個中性的 bréhman（主格 brahma）經過一個眾所周知的文法過程變為陽性的 brahmén（主格 brahma）時，它開始表示一個通曉梵（Brahman）的人、祭司種姓的一員；其次，它表示一位專司監督祭祀的祭司，但同樣也表示個人的造物主、被認為是一位個人之神的宇宙力量，與梵天（Pragapati）相同，而在後來時代則為三相神（Trimirti）之一——梵天、毘濕奴（Vishnu）與濕婆（Siva）。以上為豪格博士的見解。
穆爾（Dr. Muir）在其《梵文文獻》一書中（第一冊，第240頁）從 bréhman（禱詞、讚歌之義）出發，而將其衍生的陽性詞 brahman 理解為祈禱者、詩人或智者之意，進而引申為通常的祭司，最後演變為專司特定職責的祭司。
羅特教授同樣認為梵天（Brahman）的本義是祈禱，但不是讚美或感謝，而是那種帶有意志力量指向神祇的祈求——欲將神吸引至信徒面前，並藉此獲得神的恩賜。
我必須坦承，我們現在閱讀的《吠陀》讚歌，其實並不充滿熾熱的虔敬，以至於可以稱之為情感的迸發。而且總有一個問題懸而未決，即為什麼宇宙的創造力應當以同一名稱來稱呼。在我看來，創造力或推動力的概念似乎本可以用

梵（Brahman），衍生自詞根 barh，意為破裂而出或驅動而出；但另一個梵（brahman），在其演變為讚美詩或祈禱之前，似乎具有言語或詞語的更廣泛涵義。實際上，仍有若干跡象表明詞根 barh 具有發出聲音或說話的涵義。婆羅賀婆蒂（Brihas-pati），又稱為梵天主（Brahmamnas-pati），常被解釋為言語之主（Vasas-pati），從而 brih 似乎是 vak（言語）的同義詞。但更為重要的是，拉丁文 verbum（詞語），如我多年前所指出的，可以逐字追溯至同一詞根。更有甚者，若我們接受 vridh 作為 vrih 的平行形式，英文詞彙也可以聲稱具有同樣的起源。因此似乎可以推斷，brāhman 原意為發出、詞語，隨後才引申為讚美詩、聖言及吠陀（Veda），而當其用於創造性力量之義時，則原初被理解為那發出、拋出或彰顯者。雖然頗具吸引力，但我們幾難推測梵文語言的古老制定者曾對斯多葛派的現實化邏各斯（Logos prophorikes）與內在邏各斯（Logos endiatthetos）或作為造物主之言語或思想的世界邏各斯（Logos）有任何認識。但梵（brahman）原本意為言語的某種記憶確實存在，可由吠陀的若干篇章證明。我對如《梨俱吠陀》129 篇「言語即梵」（vag vai Brahma）的篇章不予重視，蓋此處梵與呼吸（prana）、心意（manas）、太陽（aditya）及許多其他事物相同方式地相互認同。但當

真正的不朽性。243 讀梨俱吠陀 1. 164, 35，Brahma ayém vakih paramdim vyéma，如果梵天（brahman）在此被稱為最高的天堂，或者說最高的言語之織（highest woof of speech），而梵天（bréhman）與瓦爾（val）之間沒有某種關聯，那又如何能有其意義呢？在一首獻給言語之主婆利哈斯帕蒂（Brihaspati）的頌歌中，還有另一段重要的段落，我們讀到 X. 71, 1：「婆利哈斯帕蒂（言語之主，brih 或言語的主人），當人們賦予名稱，發送言語之初始時，那麼凡是他們之中最好的、最無瑕疵的，隱藏在他們內部的，便藉著渴望而顯現出來。」因此我相信梵天（brahman）這個詞有著雙重的歷史——一條開始於梵天（brahman，中性），τὸ ὄντως ὄν，宇宙的推動力量，並逐漸導向梵天（Brahman，陽性），世界的創造者，使萬物迸發而出，後來成為印度三主神（Trimrti）之一，包括梵天（Brahman）、濕婆（Siva）和毗濕奴（Vishnu）；另一條開始於梵語（bréh-man，言語或言辭），逐漸限定為梵歌（brahman，讚美之詩），伴隨著祭祀供奉，然後隨著性別和重音的改變，演變為梵人（brahman，他，吟誦者、祈禱者與祭祀者），祭司階級的一員。
梵天（Brahman），即使用作中性詞時，也經常接上陽性詞形。而且有許多段落中，梵天究竟是被理解為非個人性的力量，還是個人性的存有，乃至同時是兩者，仍然令人難以確定。因此我們讀到《泰帝利耶奧義書》（Taitt. Up.）第三卷第一章第一節所言：「那由其而生出這些存有者，那由其而存活這些既生的存有者，那由其而這些存有於死亡時進入者，試圖認識那，那就是梵天（Brahman）。」
在《阿闥婆吠陀》X. 2, 25 中，我們讀到：「由誰」
這地球是由誰來整頓的，上面的天空是由誰創造的？是由誰被高舉起來的？」等等。
答案是：「大梵天為此大地而訂立秩序」，等等。
有時候梵天（Brahman）被認同為普拉那（Prana），即呼吸，如《布里哈達若普尼沙奧義書》（Brih. Ar. Up.）第三篇（s）第 9‧9 節所述：「他問道，誰是唯一的神？亞吉瓦爾基亞（Yajnavalkya）回答說：呼吸或精神，他就是梵天。」
有時又有人說普拉那（prana）、精神從梵天（Brahman）而生，如我們在《蒙德卡奧義書》II. 1, 8 中所讀：「梵天因熱力而膨脹；因此生出食物（或物質），從食物而生命息（prana）、心識等。」
然而，這個梵天（Brahman）只是眾多名稱中的一個，每一個名稱都代表著一種嘗試，旨在抵達至高存在者（Supreme Being）的概念，盡可能地擺脫一切神話的成分，擺脫純粹人性的特質，也擺脫性別或性徵。
普魯沙（Purusha）。

另一個這樣的名字是普魯沙（Purusha），原本的意思是人或人格。因此我們在《蒙德卡奧義書》（Mund. Up.）II. 1, 1-38 中讀到：「如同從熊熊燃燒的火焰中，火花飛迸而出，千般齊發，如火似火，如是從不滅者（Imperishable）中生出種種眾生，並復歸於彼。那位天界的人格者（Purusha）無有身體，彼既在內亦在外，未被創造，無呼吸亦無心識，超越於至高，永遠不滅。從彼生出呼吸（spirit）、心識及一切感官，虛空、空氣、光、水及大地——萬物之支持者。」
